title: C Function Prototypes
content:
### Function Prototypes in C

A function prototype (or forward declaration) tells the compiler about a function before its definition.

### Syntax

```c
return_type function_name(parameter_types);
```

### Example

```c
int add(int a, int b);  // prototype

int main() {
  int result = add(5, 3);  // works because prototype exists
}

int add(int a, int b) {    // definition
  return a + b;
}
```

### Why Use Prototypes?

| Benefit | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| Compiler checks | Verifies correct function calls |
| Implicit declarations avoided | Prevents old-style K&R behavior |
| Organization | Functions can be in any order |
| Header files | Prototypes go in .h files |
| Type checking | Compiler checks argument types |

### What Happens Without a Prototype?

In C89/C90, calling a function without a prototype causes an implicit declaration. The compiler assumes the function returns `int` and accepts any arguments. This is dangerous and leads to bugs.

---

> **Key Point:** Always declare function prototypes before `main()` or put them in header files. Modern C compilers warn about implicit declarations.

quiz.question: What is another name for a function prototype?
